instruction: How do I implement a proper scope guard in C++ that runs cleanup code when a scope exits, regardless of how it exits (return, exception, etc.)?
output:
<think>
A scope guard leverages RAII to guarantee cleanup. The destructor runs when the object goes out of scope, whether via normal return, exception, or early break. This is the C++ equivalent of Go's defer or Python's context manager.

The implementation needs: (1) store a callable (lambda, function pointer), (2) call it in the destructor, (3) provide a way to dismiss/cancel it (for commit/rollback patterns), (4) be move-only (no copies — cleanup should run exactly once).

C++17 class template argument deduction (CTAD) lets us write `ScopeGuard guard([]{...})` without specifying the template parameter. We also need to mark the destructor noexcept(false) carefully — if the cleanup itself throws during stack unwinding, std::terminate is called. Best practice: cleanup lambdas should be noexcept.

The Folly library (Facebook) has a production-grade ScopeGuard. The standard library added `std::experimental::scope_exit` in the Library Fundamentals TS v3. But rolling your own is educational and often sufficient.
</think>

```cpp
#include <functional>
#include <iostream>
#include <utility>
#include <mutex>
#include <fstream>

// Generic scope guard — runs cleanup on scope exit
template<typename F>
class ScopeGuard {
    F func_;
    bool active_;
public:
    explicit ScopeGuard(F f) : func_(std::move(f)), active_(true) {}
    ~ScopeGuard() {
        if (active_) {
            try { func_(); }
            catch (...) {} // never throw from destructor
        }
    }
    void dismiss() { active_ = false; } // cancel cleanup
    
    ScopeGuard(ScopeGuard&& other) noexcept
        : func_(std::move(other.func_)), active_(other.active_) {
        other.active_ = false;
    }
    ScopeGuard(const ScopeGuard&) = delete;
    ScopeGuard& operator=(const ScopeGuard&) = delete;
    ScopeGuard& operator=(ScopeGuard&&) = delete;
};

// Helper for CTAD — deduction guide
template<typename F>
ScopeGuard(F) -> ScopeGuard<F>;

// Convenience factory
template<typename F>
auto make_guard(F&& f) {
    return ScopeGuard<std::decay_t<F>>(std::forward<F>(f));
}

// === Usage examples ===

void database_transaction() {
    std::cout << "BEGIN transaction\n";
    
    // Rollback on ANY scope exit (exception, early return)
    auto rollback = make_guard([]{
        std::cout << "ROLLBACK transaction\n";
    });
    
    // Do work that might throw...
    bool success = true; // simulate
    if (!success) return; // rollback runs
    
    // If we reach here, commit instead
    rollback.dismiss(); // cancel rollback
    std::cout << "COMMIT transaction\n";
}

void file_operations() {
    std::ofstream file("temp.txt");
    auto cleanup = make_guard([&]{
        file.close();
        std::remove("temp.txt"); // delete temp file on exit
    });
    
    file << "temporary data";
    // cleanup runs when scope exits
}

void lock_example() {
    std::mutex mtx;
    mtx.lock();
    auto unlock = make_guard([&]{ mtx.unlock(); });
    
    // Do work under lock...
    // unlock guaranteed even if exception thrown
}

int main() {
    database_transaction();
    file_operations();
    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Show me RAII patterns for managing file handles, network sockets, and database connections in modern C++. I want proper cleanup ordering when multiple resources are involved.
output:
<think>
When multiple RAII objects exist in the same scope, C++ destroys them in reverse order of construction. This is critical for resource cleanup ordering — if resource B depends on resource A, construct A first so it's destroyed last.

For files, `std::fstream` is already RAII. For sockets and DB connections, you typically wrap a C handle (file descriptor, HANDLE, connection pointer) in a class with a destructor. The key rules: (1) delete copy constructor/assignment (prevent double-free), (2) implement move constructor/assignment (transfer ownership), (3) destructor checks for valid handle before cleanup.

The unique_ptr with custom deleter pattern is powerful for wrapping C APIs. `unique_ptr<FILE, decltype(&fclose)>` manages a FILE* with fclose as the deleter. This avoids writing a full wrapper class for simple cases.

Cleanup ordering matters when: a transaction depends on a connection, a prepared statement depends on a session, a write buffer must flush before the socket closes. Construction order determines destruction order.
</think>

```cpp
#include <memory>
#include <stdexcept>
#include <iostream>
#include <cstdio>
#include <string>

// === Pattern 1: unique_ptr with custom deleter for C APIs ===
auto open_file(const char* path, const char* mode) {
    auto deleter = [](FILE* f) {
        if (f) {
            std::cout << "  Closing file\n";
            std::fclose(f);
        }
    };
    std::unique_ptr<FILE, decltype(deleter)> file(std::fopen(path, mode), deleter);
    if (!file) throw std::runtime_error("Failed to open file");
    return file;
}

// === Pattern 2: Full RAII wrapper class ===
class Connection {
    int handle_;  // simulated OS handle
    std::string name_;
public:
    explicit Connection(std::string name)
        : handle_(42), name_(std::move(name)) {
        std::cout << "  Connect: " << name_ << "\n";
    }
    ~Connection() {
        if (handle_ >= 0) {
            std::cout << "  Disconnect: " << name_ << "\n";
            handle_ = -1;
        }
    }
    // Move-only: transfer ownership
    Connection(Connection&& other) noexcept
        : handle_(other.handle_), name_(std::move(other.name_)) {
        other.handle_ = -1;
    }
    Connection& operator=(Connection&& other) noexcept {
        if (this != &other) {
            if (handle_ >= 0) { /* close old */ }
            handle_ = other.handle_;
            name_ = std::move(other.name_);
            other.handle_ = -1;
        }
        return *this;
    }
    Connection(const Connection&) = delete;
    Connection& operator=(const Connection&) = delete;
    
    int handle() const { return handle_; }
    const std::string& name() const { return name_; }
};

// === Pattern 3: Cleanup ordering ===
void multi_resource_example() {
    std::cout << "=== Constructing (order matters) ===\n";
    
    // Construct in dependency order:
    Connection db("PostgreSQL");         // 1st constructed, LAST destroyed
    Connection cache("Redis");           // 2nd constructed, 2nd destroyed
    auto logfile = open_file("app.log", "w"); // 3rd constructed, 1st destroyed
    
    std::cout << "=== Doing work ===\n";
    std::fprintf(logfile.get(), "Connected to %s\n", db.name().c_str());
    
    std::cout << "=== Scope exit — destruction in REVERSE order ===\n";
    // Destroys: logfile (flush+close), cache (disconnect), db (disconnect)
    // This is correct: file closes before connections drop
}

// === Pattern 4: RAII with commit/rollback ===
class Transaction {
    Connection& conn_;
    bool committed_ = false;
public:
    explicit Transaction(Connection& conn) : conn_(conn) {
        std::cout << "  BEGIN on " << conn_.name() << "\n";
    }
    void commit() {
        std::cout << "  COMMIT on " << conn_.name() << "\n";
        committed_ = true;
    }
    ~Transaction() {
        if (!committed_) {
            std::cout << "  ROLLBACK on " << conn_.name() << "\n";
        }
    }
};

int main() {
    multi_resource_example();
    
    std::cout << "\n=== Transaction RAII ===\n";
    Connection db("MySQL");
    {
        Transaction txn(db);
        // If exception here -> rollback in destructor
        txn.commit(); // explicit commit
    } // txn destroyed, but already committed
    return 0;
}
```

instruction: What's the modern C++ way to ensure exception-safe resource management when constructors can throw and multiple resources need to be acquired?
output:
<think>
The classic problem: a constructor acquires resource A, then tries to acquire resource B. If B's acquisition throws, the constructor never completes, so the destructor never runs, and resource A leaks.

The solution is the "member initialization" pattern: wrap each resource in its own RAII type (unique_ptr, etc.) as class members. If the constructor throws partway through, already-constructed members are destroyed automatically. This is guaranteed by C++ — member destructors run for all fully-constructed members even if the constructor throws.

The anti-pattern is raw `new` in constructor bodies with manual cleanup in catch blocks. This is fragile and doesn't compose.

Another subtlety: `make_unique` is exception-safe because it does the allocation and constructor in one expression. Writing `func(unique_ptr<A>(new A), unique_ptr<B>(new B))` had a sequencing issue before C++17 (new A could run, then new B throws before unique_ptr<A> is constructed). C++17 fixed this, but `make_unique` is still preferred.
</think>

```cpp
#include <memory>
#include <stdexcept>
#include <iostream>
#include <string>

// Simulated resources
class Database {
public:
    Database(const std::string& url) {
        std::cout << "DB connected: " << url << "\n";
    }
    ~Database() { std::cout << "DB disconnected\n"; }
};

class Cache {
public:
    Cache(const std::string& url) {
        std::cout << "Cache connected: " << url << "\n";
        // Simulate failure
        throw std::runtime_error("Cache connection refused");
    }
    ~Cache() { std::cout << "Cache disconnected\n"; }
};

class MessageQueue {
public:
    MessageQueue(const std::string& url) {
        std::cout << "MQ connected: " << url << "\n";
    }
    ~MessageQueue() { std::cout << "MQ disconnected\n"; }
};

// ❌ BAD: raw pointers — if cache_ construction throws, db_ leaks
class BadService {
    Database* db_;
    Cache* cache_;
    MessageQueue* mq_;
public:
    BadService() {
        db_ = new Database("pg://localhost");    // allocated
        cache_ = new Cache("redis://localhost"); // THROWS! db_ leaks!
        mq_ = new MessageQueue("amqp://localhost");
    }
    ~BadService() {
        delete mq_; delete cache_; delete db_;
    }
};

// ✅ GOOD: unique_ptr members — already-constructed members auto-destroy
class GoodService {
    std::unique_ptr<Database> db_;
    std::unique_ptr<Cache> cache_;
    std::unique_ptr<MessageQueue> mq_;
public:
    GoodService()
        : db_(std::make_unique<Database>("pg://localhost"))       // constructed
        , cache_(std::make_unique<Cache>("redis://localhost"))    // throws!
        , mq_(std::make_unique<MessageQueue>("amqp://localhost")) // never reached
    {
        // If cache_ throws, db_ is already constructed and its destructor runs
        // mq_ was never constructed, so its destructor does NOT run
        // Result: DB properly disconnected, no leak
    }
    // Destructor is trivial — unique_ptrs handle everything
};

int main() {
    std::cout << "=== BAD (leaks db_) ===\n";
    try {
        BadService bad; // db_ leaks when Cache throws
    } catch (const std::exception& e) {
        std::cout << "Caught: " << e.what() << "\n";
    }

    std::cout << "\n=== GOOD (no leak) ===\n";
    try {
        GoodService good; // db_ auto-cleaned when Cache throws
    } catch (const std::exception& e) {
        std::cout << "Caught: " << e.what() << "\n";
    }
    return 0;
}
```

instruction: How do I implement the RAII pattern for managing GPU resources (CUDA memory, OpenGL textures, Vulkan buffers) in C++?
output:
<think>
GPU resources are the perfect RAII use case because forgetting to free them causes GPU memory leaks that accumulate across frames/iterations and eventually crash. The pattern is the same as any RAII: allocate in constructor, free in destructor, move-only semantics.

For CUDA: `cudaMalloc` / `cudaFree`. For OpenGL: `glGenTextures` / `glDeleteTextures`. For Vulkan: `vkAllocateMemory` / `vkFreeMemory`. Each API has its own quirks, but the wrapper pattern is identical.

Key considerations: (1) GPU operations can fail — handle errors in constructors, (2) destruction order matters — destroy child resources before parent (e.g., Vulkan buffers before device), (3) some GPU APIs require the context to be current (OpenGL), (4) move semantics must null out the source to prevent double-free.

For production code, most frameworks provide their own RAII wrappers (CUDA Thrust, Vulkan-HPP unique handles). But understanding the pattern helps when wrapping custom GPU resources.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <stdexcept>
#include <cstddef>
#include <utility>

// Simulated CUDA API (replace with real cuda_runtime.h)
namespace cuda {
    enum Error { Success = 0, OutOfMemory = 2 };
    Error malloc(void** ptr, size_t size) {
        *ptr = std::malloc(size);
        return *ptr ? Success : OutOfMemory;
    }
    Error free(void* ptr) { std::free(ptr); return Success; }
    Error memcpy(void* dst, const void* src, size_t n, int kind) {
        std::memcpy(dst, src, n); return Success;
    }
    constexpr int MemcpyHostToDevice = 1;
    constexpr int MemcpyDeviceToHost = 2;
}

// RAII wrapper for GPU memory
template<typename T>
class DeviceBuffer {
    T* ptr_ = nullptr;
    size_t count_ = 0;

public:
    explicit DeviceBuffer(size_t count) : count_(count) {
        auto err = cuda::malloc(reinterpret_cast<void**>(&ptr_), count * sizeof(T));
        if (err != cuda::Success) {
            throw std::runtime_error("cudaMalloc failed");
        }
        std::cout << "  GPU alloc: " << count * sizeof(T) << " bytes\n";
    }

    ~DeviceBuffer() {
        if (ptr_) {
            cuda::free(ptr_);
            std::cout << "  GPU free: " << count_ * sizeof(T) << " bytes\n";
            ptr_ = nullptr;
        }
    }

    // Move semantics — transfer ownership
    DeviceBuffer(DeviceBuffer&& other) noexcept
        : ptr_(other.ptr_), count_(other.count_) {
        other.ptr_ = nullptr;  // null source to prevent double-free
        other.count_ = 0;
    }

    DeviceBuffer& operator=(DeviceBuffer&& other) noexcept {
        if (this != &other) {
            if (ptr_) cuda::free(ptr_);
            ptr_ = other.ptr_;
            count_ = other.count_;
            other.ptr_ = nullptr;
            other.count_ = 0;
        }
        return *this;
    }

    // No copy — GPU memory isn't implicitly copyable
    DeviceBuffer(const DeviceBuffer&) = delete;
    DeviceBuffer& operator=(const DeviceBuffer&) = delete;

    // Upload from host
    void upload(const T* host_data, size_t count) {
        cuda::memcpy(ptr_, host_data, count * sizeof(T), cuda::MemcpyHostToDevice);
    }

    // Download to host
    void download(T* host_data, size_t count) const {
        cuda::memcpy(host_data, ptr_, count * sizeof(T), cuda::MemcpyDeviceToHost);
    }

    T* data() { return ptr_; }
    const T* data() const { return ptr_; }
    size_t size() const { return count_; }
};

// RAII GPU computation — all resources cleaned up automatically
void gpu_computation() {
    std::cout << "=== Allocating GPU resources ===\n";

    DeviceBuffer<float> input(1024);    // 4KB on GPU
    DeviceBuffer<float> weights(1024);  // 4KB on GPU
    DeviceBuffer<float> output(1024);   // 4KB on GPU

    // Upload data
    std::vector<float> host_input(1024, 1.0f);
    input.upload(host_input.data(), 1024);

    // Simulate kernel launch using buffers...
    // launch_kernel(input.data(), weights.data(), output.data(), 1024);

    // Download results
    std::vector<float> host_output(1024);
    output.download(host_output.data(), 1024);

    std::cout << "=== Scope exit — GPU memory freed in reverse order ===\n";
    // output freed, then weights, then input — automatic
}

int main() {
    gpu_computation();
    // All GPU memory guaranteed freed even if exceptions occur
    return 0;
}
```
